verses.commentary: Passover and Unleavened Bread two days away. Chief priests and teachers scheme to arrest Jesus secretly and kill him. Murder planned during the feast of liberation. Irony.
But not during the festival, or the people may riot. Fear of crowds. They want quiet execution. God's plan will override theirs: Jesus dies at Passover, the Lamb of God at the feast of lambs.
In Bethany at Simon the Leper's house, a woman comes with an alabaster jar of pure nard, very expensive. She breaks the jar, pours it on his head. Royal anointing. Extravagant devotion.
Some present indignant: Why this waste of perfume?
It could have been sold for more than a year's wages, the money given to the poor. They rebuke her harshly. Economic calculation versus worship. They value money over Jesus.
Jesus: Leave her alone. Why are you bothering her? She has done a beautiful thing to me. He defends her. Beautiful (kalos, καλός): good, noble, honorable.
The poor you will always have, and you can help them anytime. But you will not always have me. Unique moment. His physical presence is temporary. Opportunities expire.
She did what she could. She poured perfume on my body beforehand to prepare for my burial. Prophetic act. She anoints him for death. She understands what the disciples miss.

Truly, wherever the gospel is preached throughout the world, what she has done will be told in memory of her. Universal, eternal memorial. Her act is inseparable from the gospel. Devotion preserved forever.
Then Judas Iscariot, one of the Twelve, went to the chief priests to betray Jesus. Starkest contrast. From extravagant love to calculated betrayal. One of the Twelve. Insider becomes traitor.
They were delighted, promised him money. He watched for an opportunity. Greed. Judas sells Jesus. The transaction is made.
First day of Unleavened Bread, when Passover lamb is sacrificed. Disciples ask: Where do you want us to prepare the Passover? Timing: Jesus, the true Lamb, will be sacrificed.
He sends two disciples: Go into the city, a man carrying a jar of water will meet you. Follow him. Unusual: men didn't typically carry water jars (women's task). Specific sign.
Say to the house owner: The Teacher asks, Where is my guest room? Prearrangement or supernatural knowledge. Either way, sovereign control.
He will show you a large upstairs room, furnished and ready. Make preparations there. Everything prepared. The stage is set for the Last Supper.
They found things just as Jesus said. They prepared the Passover. His word proven reliable again.
When evening came, Jesus arrived with the Twelve. Night falls. Darkness descends. The final meal begins.

While reclining and eating, he says: Truly, one of you will betray me, one who is eating with me. Shocking announcement. Psalm 41:9: even my close friend who ate my bread has lifted his heel against me. Ultimate betrayal: table fellowship violated.
They were saddened. One by one: Surely not I? Each suspects himself. Self-awareness or self-deception?
It is one of the Twelve, one who dips bread with me. Intimacy compounds the betrayal. Shared meal, shared treachery.

The Son of Man will go just as it is written. But woe to that man who betrays the Son of Man! It would be better if he had not been born. Sovereignty and responsibility both affirmed. Prophecy does not excuse. Judas' fate is worse than non-existence.
While eating, Jesus took bread, gave thanks, broke it, gave it to them: Take it; this is my body. Institution of the Eucharist. Bread = body. Broken for them.
He took a cup, gave thanks, gave it to them. They all drank from it. The cup shared. Unity in the covenant.

This is my blood of the covenant, poured out for many. Exodus 24:8, Jeremiah 31:31. New covenant inaugurated. His blood ratifies it. Poured out = violent death. For many, not all: particular, effective atonement.
Truly, I will not drink again from the fruit of the vine until that day when I drink it new in the kingdom of God. Future hope. Reunion feast in the consummated kingdom. This is not the end.
When they had sung a hymn, they went out to the Mount of Olives. Likely Psalms 115–118 (Hallel). Worship then crisis.
Jesus: You will all fall away, for it is written: I will strike the shepherd, and the sheep will be scattered. Zechariah 13:7. God strikes Jesus; disciples scatter. Prophesied abandonment.
But after I rise, I will go ahead of you into Galilee. Resurrection promise. Restoration promised. The scattering is not final.
Peter: Even if all fall away, I will not. Confident boast. Self-reliance. He sets himself above the others.
Jesus: Truly, today, tonight, before the rooster crows twice, you will disown me three times. Specific, detailed prediction. Peter's failure precisely foretold.
Peter insisted emphatically: Even if I have to die with you, I will never disown you. All the others said the same. Unanimous confidence. Universal failure coming.
They went to Gethsemane. Jesus: Sit here while I pray. Place of pressing (gat shmanim, oil press). Fitting: here Jesus is crushed.
He took Peter, James, John. Began to be deeply distressed and troubled. Rare window into Jesus' emotions. Horror overwhelms him. The inner three witness his agony.
My soul is overwhelmed with sorrow to the point of death. Stay here and keep watch. Deathly sorrow. He asks for their presence. Loneliness in the face of death.
Going farther, he fell to the ground, prayed that if possible the hour might pass. Prostrate. If possible: can there be another way? He faces what he came to do.

Abba, Father, everything is possible for you. Take this cup from me. Yet not what I will, but what you will. Abba: intimate Aramaic address. The cup: God's wrath (Isaiah 51:17, Psalm 75:8). Jesus asks for escape but submits to the Father's will. Perfect obedience.
He returns, finds them sleeping. Simon, are you asleep? Couldn't you keep watch for one hour? Disappointment. Peter who boasted of loyalty cannot stay awake.
Watch and pray so you will not fall into temptation. The spirit is willing, but the flesh is weak. Diagnosis. They mean well, but flesh fails. Prayer is necessary protection.
He went away and prayed the same thing. Repetition. Persistent prayer. The struggle continues.
He returned, found them sleeping, because their eyes were heavy. They did not know what to say. Exhausted. Speechless. No excuse.
The third time: Are you still sleeping and resting? Enough! The hour has come. The Son of Man is delivered into the hands of sinners. Three prayers, three failures. Time is up. Betrayal arrives.
Rise! Let us go! Here comes my betrayer! Jesus rises to meet his fate. No flight. Courageous advance.
Just as he spoke, Judas appeared with a crowd armed with swords and clubs, sent by chief priests, teachers, elders. Mob sent by religious authorities. Violence sanctioned by the holy.
The betrayer arranged a signal: The one I kiss is the man; arrest him, lead him away under guard. Kiss as betrayal sign. Intimacy weaponized.
Judas goes at once to Jesus: Rabbi! And kissed him. Greeting becomes treachery. Rabbi: honored teacher. The title mocks.
The men seized Jesus and arrested him. Hands that healed are bound.
One standing near drew a sword, struck the high priest's servant, cutting off his ear. Violent resistance. Peter identified in John 18:10. Futile.
Jesus: Am I leading a rebellion, that you come with swords and clubs? Irony. He who preached peace is arrested as an insurrectionist.
Every day I was with you, teaching in the temple, and you did not arrest me. But the Scriptures must be fulfilled. Public ministry without arrest. Now clandestine capture. All according to plan.
Then everyone deserted him and fled. Zechariah 13:7 fulfilled. Sheep scattered. Universal abandonment. Alone.
A young man, wearing only a linen garment, was following Jesus. When they seized him...
He fled naked, leaving his garment behind. Mysterious detail. Possibly Mark himself. Naked flight = total escape, shame. All dignity lost in flight.
They took Jesus to the high priest. All chief priests, elders, teachers came together. Sanhedrin assembled. Illegal night trial.
Peter followed at a distance, right into the high priest's courtyard. Sat with guards, warmed himself. Courage and cowardice. He follows, but from afar. Physical proximity, spiritual distance.
Chief priests and Sanhedrin looked for evidence against Jesus to put him to death, but found none. Verdict before trial. They search for justification for predetermined conclusion.
Many testified falsely, but their statements did not agree. False witnesses contradict each other. Truth is cohesive; lies fragment.
Some stood up, gave false testimony.

We heard him say: I will destroy this temple made with hands, in three days will build another, not made with hands. Distortion of John 2:19–21. He spoke of his body, not Herod's temple. Truth twisted into accusation.
Yet even then their testimony did not agree. Incompetent conspiracy. They cannot even lie consistently.
The high priest stood, asked Jesus: Are you not going to answer? What is this testimony against you? Demand for response.

But Jesus remained silent, gave no answer. Isaiah 53:7: He was oppressed and afflicted, yet he did not open his mouth. Sovereign silence. Then the high priest asked: Are you the Messiah, the Son of the Blessed One? The question. Identity on trial.

Jesus: I am. And you will see the Son of Man sitting at the right hand of the Mighty One and coming on the clouds of heaven. Unambiguous claim. I am (Ego eimi, Ἐγώ εἰμι): divine name. Daniel 7:13, Psalm 110:1. Messiah, divine judge, coming king. Full disclosure.
The high priest tore his clothes. Why do we need more witnesses? Tearing garments = response to blasphemy. He has his verdict.
You have heard the blasphemy. What do you think? They all condemned him as worthy of death. Unanimous verdict. The sinless one condemned by sinners.
Some began to spit on him, blindfold him, strike him with fists: Prophesy! Guards beat him. Mockery. Isaiah 50:6 fulfilled. The prophet is told to prophesy by those who reject prophecy.
While Peter was below in the courtyard, one of the high priest's servant girls came by. The scene shifts. Jesus' trial, Peter's trial.
She saw Peter warming himself, looked closely: You were with that Nazarene, Jesus. Recognition. Simple question from a powerless girl.
He denied: I don't know or understand what you're talking about. First denial. He went to the entryway. Flight begins.
The servant girl saw him, said to those around: This fellow is one of them. Persistent identification.
Again he denied. After a while, those standing near said: Surely you are one of them, for you are a Galilean. Accent betrays. Geography convicts.
He began to call down curses, swore to them: I don't know this man you're talking about. Third denial. Curses and oaths. Emphatic repudiation. This man: distancing language.

Immediately the rooster crowed the second time. Peter remembered Jesus' word: Before the rooster crows twice you will disown me three times. And he broke down and wept. Prophecy fulfilled. Memory floods back. Broken. Weeping. Repentance begins. Chapter fourteen: anointing, betrayal, Last Supper, Gethsemane, arrest, trial, denial. The passion accelerates. Jesus moves toward the cross; the disciples scatter. Love and betrayal, courage and cowardice, prophecy and fulfillment. The King is condemned, the rock crumbles, the Son of Man is delivered to death.
